title: Hypertension: diagnosis and initial management
text: Hypertension is diagnosed when office blood pressure is persistently 140/90 mmHg or higher on repeated readings, or when out-of-office monitoring confirms elevated values. Stage 1 hypertension is 140-159/90-99 mmHg and stage 2 is 160/100 mmHg or higher. Initial evaluation should include assessment of cardiovascular risk factors, basic metabolic panel, urinalysis, lipid profile, and an ECG. First-line lifestyle measures are sodium restriction, weight loss, the DASH diet, regular aerobic exercise, and limiting alcohol. First-line antihypertensive drug classes include thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers, and calcium channel blockers.

title: Hypertensive urgency versus emergency
text: A hypertensive emergency is severe blood pressure elevation, usually above 180/120 mmHg, accompanied by acute target-organ damage such as encephalopathy, intracranial hemorrhage, acute heart failure, aortic dissection, or acute kidney injury. It requires admission and controlled intravenous blood pressure reduction. Hypertensive urgency is severe elevation without acute organ damage and can usually be managed with oral agents and close outpatient follow-up. Blood pressure should not be lowered too rapidly because abrupt reductions can cause cerebral or coronary hypoperfusion.

title: Type 2 diabetes: diagnostic criteria
text: Type 2 diabetes is diagnosed by a fasting plasma glucose of 126 mg/dL or higher, a 2-hour plasma glucose of 200 mg/dL or higher during an oral glucose tolerance test, a hemoglobin A1c of 6.5 percent or higher, or a random glucose of 200 mg/dL or higher with classic symptoms. In the absence of unequivocal hyperglycemia, an abnormal result should be confirmed by repeat testing. Prediabetes is defined by A1c 5.7 to 6.4 percent or impaired fasting glucose of 100 to 125 mg/dL.

title: Type 2 diabetes: glucose-lowering therapy
text: Metformin is the preferred initial pharmacologic therapy for type 2 diabetes unless contraindicated, alongside lifestyle modification. In patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor or a GLP-1 receptor agonist with proven benefit is recommended regardless of A1c. Metformin should be used cautiously and dose-adjusted when the estimated GFR is between 30 and 45 mL/min and is contraindicated below 30. A general A1c target is below 7 percent, individualized by age, comorbidity, and hypoglycemia risk.

title: Diabetic ketoacidosis recognition
text: Diabetic ketoacidosis is characterized by hyperglycemia, an anion-gap metabolic acidosis, and ketonemia or ketonuria. Typical features include polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and deep rapid Kussmaul breathing. Management centers on intravenous fluid resuscitation, an insulin infusion, and careful replacement of potassium, because total-body potassium is depleted even when serum levels appear normal. Precipitants include infection, missed insulin, and new-onset diabetes.

title: Asthma: diagnosis and control
text: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease causing variable expiratory airflow limitation. Diagnosis is supported by a history of episodic wheeze, cough, chest tightness, and breathlessness, together with demonstrated reversible airflow obstruction on spirometry, typically an increase in FEV1 of at least 12 percent after a bronchodilator. Inhaled corticosteroids are the cornerstone of controller therapy. Short-acting beta-2 agonists relieve acute symptoms, and reliance on frequent reliever use indicates poor control.

title: Acute asthma exacerbation management
text: An acute asthma exacerbation is treated with repeated inhaled short-acting beta-2 agonists, early systemic corticosteroids, and controlled oxygen to maintain saturation around 93 to 95 percent. Inhaled ipratropium is added in severe cases. Signs of a life-threatening attack include a silent chest, cyanosis, exhaustion, altered consciousness, and a normalizing or rising carbon dioxide level, which should prompt consideration of intensive care and ventilatory support.

title: COPD diagnosis and management
text: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is defined by persistent respiratory symptoms and airflow limitation confirmed by a post-bronchodilator FEV1 to FVC ratio below 0.70. The main risk factor is tobacco smoking. Management includes smoking cessation, which is the single most effective intervention, inhaled bronchodilators such as long-acting muscarinic antagonists and long-acting beta-2 agonists, pulmonary rehabilitation, and vaccination against influenza and pneumococcus. Inhaled corticosteroids are added for frequent exacerbations or eosinophilia.

title: Acute coronary syndrome and chest pain
text: Acute coronary syndrome should be suspected in patients with chest pain that is pressure-like, may radiate to the arm, neck, or jaw, and is associated with diaphoresis, dyspnea, or nausea. Immediate evaluation includes a 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes and serial cardiac troponin measurements. ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction requires urgent reperfusion with primary percutaneous coronary intervention or, if unavailable, fibrinolysis. Initial medical therapy includes aspirin, and nitrates for ongoing ischemic pain in the absence of contraindications.

title: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
text: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction is defined by a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40 percent or less with signs and symptoms of heart failure such as dyspnea, orthopnea, fatigue, and peripheral edema. Guideline-directed medical therapy includes an ACE inhibitor, ARB, or angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor, a beta-blocker proven in heart failure, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Diuretics are used to relieve congestion but do not improve survival.

title: Atrial fibrillation and stroke prevention
text: Atrial fibrillation is an irregularly irregular supraventricular arrhythmia that increases the risk of thromboembolic stroke. Stroke risk is estimated with the CHA2DS2-VASc score, and oral anticoagulation is recommended when the score is 2 or higher in men or 3 or higher in women. Direct oral anticoagulants are preferred over warfarin for most patients with non-valvular atrial fibrillation. Rate control and rhythm control strategies are chosen based on symptoms and patient factors.

title: Deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism
text: Deep vein thrombosis commonly presents with unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema. Pretest probability is assessed with the Wells score, and a D-dimer is useful to rule out disease in low-probability patients, while compression ultrasound confirms the diagnosis. Pulmonary embolism may present with acute dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and tachycardia. Anticoagulation is the mainstay of treatment, and hemodynamically unstable pulmonary embolism may require thrombolysis.

title: Acute ischemic stroke
text: Acute ischemic stroke presents with sudden focal neurological deficits such as facial droop, arm weakness, and speech difficulty. Immediate non-contrast CT of the head is performed to exclude hemorrhage. Intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase or tenecteplase may be given within 4.5 hours of symptom onset in eligible patients, and mechanical thrombectomy is indicated for large-vessel occlusion within an extended window in selected patients. Time from onset is the critical determinant of eligibility.

title: Community-acquired pneumonia
text: Community-acquired pneumonia presents with fever, productive cough, dyspnea, and pleuritic chest pain, with focal crackles on examination and an infiltrate on chest radiograph. Severity and site of care are guided by tools such as CURB-65. Empiric outpatient therapy in previously healthy adults commonly uses amoxicillin or a macrolide or doxycycline, while inpatients receive a beta-lactam plus a macrolide or a respiratory fluoroquinolone. Antibiotic choice should reflect local resistance patterns.

title: Urinary tract infection in adults
text: Uncomplicated cystitis presents with dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort without fever or flank pain. First-line treatment options include nitrofurantoin, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole where resistance is low, or fosfomycin. Pyelonephritis is suggested by fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness and requires longer courses and sometimes hospitalization. Asymptomatic bacteriuria should generally not be treated except in pregnancy or before urologic procedures.

title: Sepsis recognition and initial bundle
text: Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. Early recognition uses signs of infection with organ dysfunction, and the quick SOFA criteria highlight altered mentation, respiratory rate of 22 or more, and systolic blood pressure of 100 mmHg or less. The initial bundle includes measuring lactate, obtaining blood cultures before antibiotics, administering broad-spectrum antibiotics promptly, and giving intravenous crystalloid for hypotension or elevated lactate. Vasopressors are added for persistent hypotension.

title: Hypothyroidism
text: Primary hypothyroidism is characterized by an elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone with a low free thyroxine level. Common symptoms are fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, and bradycardia. The most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions is autoimmune Hashimoto thyroiditis. Treatment is levothyroxine replacement titrated to normalize the thyroid-stimulating hormone, with dose adjustments guided by repeat testing after about six weeks.

title: Hyperthyroidism and Graves disease
text: Hyperthyroidism presents with weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, and a suppressed thyroid-stimulating hormone with elevated thyroid hormone levels. Graves disease is the most common cause and may include ophthalmopathy. Treatment options include antithyroid drugs such as methimazole, radioactive iodine, and surgery. Beta-blockers relieve adrenergic symptoms. Propylthiouracil is preferred in the first trimester of pregnancy and in thyroid storm.

title: Iron deficiency anemia
text: Iron deficiency anemia produces a microcytic hypochromic anemia with low ferritin, low serum iron, elevated total iron-binding capacity, and low transferrin saturation. Causes include chronic blood loss from the gastrointestinal or genitourinary tract, poor intake, and malabsorption. In older adults and postmenopausal women, gastrointestinal evaluation for a bleeding source is important. Treatment is oral iron replacement, with intravenous iron reserved for intolerance or malabsorption.

title: Chronic kidney disease staging
text: Chronic kidney disease is defined by markers of kidney damage or a glomerular filtration rate below 60 mL/min per 1.73 m2 persisting for at least three months. It is staged by GFR category from G1 to G5 and by albuminuria category. Management focuses on blood pressure control, use of ACE inhibitors or ARBs when albuminuria is present, SGLT2 inhibitors for cardiorenal protection, avoidance of nephrotoxins, and monitoring for complications such as anemia and mineral bone disease.

title: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
text: Gastroesophageal reflux disease causes heartburn and acid regurgitation and can produce cough, hoarseness, and chest discomfort. Lifestyle measures include weight loss, avoiding late meals, and elevating the head of the bed. Proton pump inhibitors are the most effective medical therapy. Alarm features such as dysphagia, weight loss, gastrointestinal bleeding, or anemia warrant endoscopy to exclude complications like stricture or Barrett esophagus.

title: Peptic ulcer disease and H. pylori
text: Peptic ulcer disease is most often caused by Helicobacter pylori infection or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug use. It presents with epigastric pain that may relate to meals, and complications include bleeding and perforation. Testing for H. pylori uses the urea breath test, stool antigen, or biopsy, and eradication uses combination antibiotic and proton pump inhibitor regimens. Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs should be stopped when possible.

title: Migraine diagnosis and treatment
text: Migraine is a recurrent headache disorder with attacks lasting 4 to 72 hours, typically unilateral, pulsating, moderate to severe, and worsened by activity, often with nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Acute treatment includes nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and triptans. Preventive therapy is considered for frequent or disabling attacks and includes beta-blockers, topiramate, amitriptyline, and CGRP-targeted therapies. Red-flag features suggesting a secondary cause require urgent imaging.

title: Major depressive disorder
text: Major depressive disorder is diagnosed when at least five of nine symptoms, including depressed mood or loss of interest, are present most of the day nearly every day for at least two weeks and cause functional impairment. Screening tools include the PHQ-9. First-line treatments include psychotherapy and antidepressants such as selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors. Suicide risk should be assessed at every visit, and response is typically reviewed after several weeks.

title: Generalized anxiety disorder
text: Generalized anxiety disorder involves excessive, difficult-to-control worry occurring more days than not for at least six months, with symptoms such as restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, muscle tension, and sleep disturbance. First-line treatments are cognitive behavioral therapy and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors or serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors. Benzodiazepines are generally avoided for long-term use because of dependence and cognitive effects.

title: Osteoporosis screening and treatment
text: Osteoporosis is diagnosed by a bone mineral density T-score of minus 2.5 or lower at the hip or spine, or by a fragility fracture. Screening with dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry is recommended for women aged 65 and older and younger women with risk factors. Management includes adequate calcium and vitamin D, weight-bearing exercise, fall prevention, and pharmacologic therapy, with bisphosphonates as common first-line agents. Fracture risk can be estimated using the FRAX tool.

title: Gout: acute and chronic management
text: Gout is caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition and presents as acute monoarticular arthritis, classically of the first metatarsophalangeal joint, with sudden severe pain, swelling, and redness. Acute attacks are treated with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, colchicine, or corticosteroids. Long-term urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol is indicated for recurrent attacks, tophi, or urate nephrolithiasis, targeting a serum urate below 6 mg/dL.

title: Rheumatoid arthritis
text: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic autoimmune inflammatory arthritis causing symmetric small-joint pain, swelling, and prolonged morning stiffness. Serologic markers include rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies, and inflammatory markers are often elevated. Early initiation of disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs, with methotrexate as the anchor drug, improves outcomes and prevents joint destruction. Biologic agents are added when disease activity remains high.

title: Hyperlipidemia and statin therapy
text: Management of blood cholesterol focuses on reducing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk. High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for those with an LDL cholesterol of 190 mg/dL or higher. For primary prevention, risk is estimated with a pooled cohort equation, and statin therapy is guided by risk category. Lifestyle changes including diet and exercise are advised for all patients, and ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitors may be added.

title: Hepatitis B overview
text: Hepatitis B is a viral infection of the liver transmitted through blood, sexual contact, and from mother to child. Acute infection may be asymptomatic or cause jaundice, fatigue, and right upper quadrant pain, and a portion of infections become chronic, raising the risk of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. Serologic markers distinguish acute, chronic, resolved, and vaccinated states. Vaccination is highly effective, and antiviral therapy is available for chronic infection.

title: Tuberculosis: latent and active
text: Tuberculosis is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and primarily affects the lungs, causing chronic cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Latent infection is detected by a tuberculin skin test or interferon-gamma release assay without active disease, and active pulmonary disease is confirmed by sputum testing including nucleic acid amplification and culture. Active disease is treated with multidrug regimens over several months, and directly observed therapy improves adherence.

title: HIV screening and antiretroviral therapy
text: HIV infection is diagnosed with combination antigen and antibody immunoassays confirmed by supplemental testing. Routine screening is recommended for adolescents and adults at least once, with more frequent testing for those at higher risk. Antiretroviral therapy is recommended for all people with HIV regardless of CD4 count, improving survival and preventing transmission. Preexposure prophylaxis reduces the risk of acquiring HIV in people at substantial risk.

title: Hypoglycemia in diabetes
text: Hypoglycemia in people treated for diabetes is defined by a low plasma glucose with symptoms such as sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, confusion, and in severe cases seizures or loss of consciousness. Conscious patients are treated with 15 to 20 grams of fast-acting oral carbohydrate, repeated after 15 minutes if still low. Severe hypoglycemia in an unconscious patient is treated with intramuscular glucagon or intravenous dextrose. Recurrent episodes warrant regimen review.

title: COPD exacerbation
text: A COPD exacerbation is an acute worsening of respiratory symptoms with increased dyspnea, cough, and sputum volume or purulence. Management includes inhaled short-acting bronchodilators, systemic corticosteroids, and antibiotics when there is increased sputum purulence or a need for ventilatory support. Controlled oxygen targets a saturation of about 88 to 92 percent to avoid carbon dioxide retention, and noninvasive ventilation is used for respiratory acidosis.

title: Pulmonary embolism risk stratification
text: After pulmonary embolism is confirmed, risk stratification guides treatment intensity. Hemodynamic instability defines high-risk pulmonary embolism and may warrant systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy. Intermediate-risk patients have right ventricular strain on imaging or elevated cardiac biomarkers but stable blood pressure. Most patients are treated with anticoagulation, and direct oral anticoagulants are commonly used. Duration depends on whether the event was provoked or unprovoked.

title: Cellulitis
text: Cellulitis is a bacterial skin and soft-tissue infection presenting with spreading erythema, warmth, swelling, and tenderness, usually of a lower limb, sometimes with fever. Most cases are caused by streptococci and Staphylococcus aureus. Uncomplicated cellulitis is treated with oral antibiotics targeting these organisms, while systemic toxicity, rapid spread, or failure of oral therapy warrants intravenous treatment. Marking the border helps monitor response.
